coredata.dc:title: Electronic coupling between molybdenum disulfide and gold nanoparticles to enhance the peroxidase activity for the colorimetric immunoassays of hydrogen peroxide and cancer cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Haohao
Gao, Yan
Hu, Narisu
Zhang, Yongxia
Guo, Chongshen
Gao, Guanggang
Ma, Zhuo
Ivan Ivanovich, Krasnyuk
Qiu, Yunfeng
coredata.dc:description: Peroxidase nanoenzymes exhibit a specific affinity toward substrates, thereby demonstrating application potential for realizing the colorimetric immunoassays of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which can be further used as a probe for imaging cancer cells. To enhance the intrinsic peroxidase activity of molybdenum sulfide (MoS2) nanomaterials, gold (Au) nanoparticles with an average diameter of approximately 2.1 nm were modified on a MoS2/carbon surface (denoted as MoS2/C-Au600) via ascorbic acid reduction. MoS2/C-Au600 can oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to generate a blue oxidation product in the presence of H2O2; this product exhibits peroxidase-like activities, superior to those of most existing MoS2-based nanoenzymes. Furthermore, MoS2/C-Au600 exhibits a high detection capability for H2O2 in the range of 1 × 10−5 to 2 × 10−4 mol/L (R2 = 0.99), and the lowest detection limit is 1.82 µmol/L in a sodium acetate and acetic acid buffer solution. Steady state kinetics studies indicate that the catalytic mechanism is consistent with the ping-pong mechanism. Given its strong absorption peak at 652 nm in the visible region, MoS2/C-Au600 can be used to image cancer cells due to the enhanced permeability and retention effect. Our findings demonstrate that the synergistic electronic coupling between multiple components can enhance the peroxidase activity, which can facilitate the development of an effective, facile, and reliable method to perform colorimetric immunoassays of H2O2 and cancer cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and spectra characterizations of catalysts. Low and high resolution TEM images of (a and b) MoS2/C nanospheres and (c and d) MoS2/C-Au600 nanospheres. (e) Raman spectra and (f) XRD patterns of MoS2/C and MoS2/C-Au600 nanospheres, respectively.
XPS measurements of MoS2/C and MoS2/C-Au600. (a) Full XPS spectra. High resolution XPS spectra of (b) Mo 3d, (c) S 2p, and (d) Au 4f.

Peroxidase-like activity evaluation. Dependence of the peroxidase-like activity on the (a) pH value (2, 3, 4, 5, and 6) and (b) temperature (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, and 70 °C). (c) UV–vis spectra of TMB solutions in six catalytic systems including (1) TMB, (2) TMB + H2O2, (3) MoS2/C + TMB, (4) MoS2/C-Au600 + TMB, (5) MoS2/C + TMB + H2O2, and (6) MoS2/C-Au600 + TMB + H2O2. The insets show the corresponding images of the solution color. (d) Time dependent absorbance changes at 652 nm in the different reaction systems. The concentrations of the nanoenzyme, TMB and H2O2 were 0.1 mg mL−1, 0.5 mM and 10 mM, respectively. The results in a, b and d correspond to the mean values of three simultaneous experiments, with the ± standard deviations represented by the error bars.

Steady state kinetic assay and catalytic mechanism of MoS2/C nanospheres. (a) The H2O2 concentration was 10 mM, and the TMB concentration was varied from 0.05 to 2.5 mM. (b) The TMB concentration was 0.5 mM, and the H2O2 concentration was varied from 1 to 35 mM. (c and d) Double reciprocal plots of the activity of MoS2/C nanospheres when the concentration of one substrate was fixed and that of the other substrate was varied. The results correspond to the mean values obtained in three parallel experiments, and the ± standard deviations are represented by error bars.

Steady state kinetic assay and catalytic mechanism of MoS2/C-Au600. (a) The concentration of H2O2 was 10 mM, and the TMB concentration was varied from 0.05 to 2.5 mM. (b) The concentration of TMB was 0.5 mM, and the H2O2 concentration was varied from 1 to 35 mM. (c and d) Double reciprocal plots of the activity of MoS2/C-Au600 when the concentration of one substrate was fixed and that of the other substrate was varied. The results correspond to the mean values obtained in three parallel experiments, and the ± standard deviations are represented by error bars.

Effect of MoS2/C-Au600 on the formation of the hydroxyl radical, using TA as a fluorescent probe. (a) Chemical equation for the HTA formation. (b) Fluorescence spectra of different reaction systems: (1) MoS2/C-Au600 + TA; (2) TA; (3) H2O2 + TA; (4) MoS2/C-Au600 + H2O2 + TA. The reaction is performed in a 0.2 M acetate buffer (pH = 4.0) containing 0.45 mM TA, at 60 °C for 10 min.

Detection of H2O2. The UV–vis spectra change as a function of the H2O2 concentrations in the catalytic systems of (a) MoS2/C + TMB + H2O2 and (d) MoS2/C-Au600 + TMB + H2O2, respectively. (b and e) Corresponding plots of the absorbance intensities vs·H2O2 concentrations. (c and f) Corresponding linear calibration plots for the H2O2 concentrations. Reaction conditions: 0.5 mM TMB and 0.1 mg mL−1 nanoenzymes were added in 0.2 M acetate buffer (pH 4.0) at 60 °C for 5 min, under various H2O2 concentrations. The results in b, c, e and f correspond to the mean values obtained in three parallel experiments, with the ± standard deviations represented by error bars.
Detection of HepG2 cells (a) without and (with) MoS2/C-Au600. Both experiments were performed in the presence of TMB and H2O2.
(a) Schematic of the synthesis process for MoS2/C-Au nanospheres. (b) Schematic of the colorimetric detection of H2O2 catalyzed by MoS2/C-Au nanospheres.
Plausible mechanism for the improved peroxidase-like activity of MoS2/C-Au600.

Comparison of Michaelis–Menten parameters for our catalysts and previous representative enzyme mimics. The results for the MoS2/C and MoS2/C-Au600 nanospheres correspond to the mean values obtained in three parallel experiments.

Comparison of linear range and LOD values of MoS2/C-Au600 with other peroxidase mimics to detect H2O2 using TMB as a substrate. The LOD of the MoS2/C and MoS2/C-Au600 nanospheres corresponded to the mean values obtained in three parallel experiments.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Peroxidase nanoenzymes exhibit a specific affinity toward substrates, thereby demonstrating application potential for realizing the colorimetric immunoassays of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which can be further used as a probe for imaging cancer cells. To enhance the intrinsic peroxidase activity of molybdenum sulfide (MoS2) nanomaterials, gold (Au) nanoparticles with an average diameter of approximately 2.1 nm were modified on a MoS2/carbon surface (denoted as MoS2/C-Au600) via ascorbic acid reduction. MoS2/C-Au600 can oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to generate a blue oxidation product in the presence of H2O2; this product exhibits peroxidase-like activities, superior to those of most existing MoS2-based nanoenzymes. Furthermore, MoS2/C-Au600 exhibits a high detection capability for H2O2 in the range of 1 × 10−5 to 2 × 10−4 mol/L (R2 = 0.99), and the lowest detection limit is 1.82 µmol/L in a sodium acetate and acetic acid buffer solution. Steady state kinetics studies indicate that the catalytic mechanism is consistent with the ping-pong mechanism. Given its strong absorption peak at 652 nm in the visible region, MoS2/C-Au600 can be used to image cancer cells due to the enhanced permeability and retention effect. Our findings demonstrate that the synergistic electronic coupling between multiple components can enhance the peroxidase activity, which can facilitate the development of an effective, facile, and reliable method to perform colorimetric immunoassays of H2O2 and cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Life phenomena in living organisms are associated with the catalytic reactions of enzymes, which can be used as probes in the biosensor and biosynthesis domain [1]. For instance, horseradish peroxidase (HRP), which is widely used to detect hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), exhibits unfavorable characteristics under harsh conditions [2]. Owing to their poor tolerance toward chemical conditions and high temperature, natural enzymes are easily denatured [3,4]. In addition, the purification requirements and high cost limit the wide application of such enzymes [5–7]. In recent years, researchers have attempted to develop nanoenzymes on the basis of nanomaterials, which exhibit unique enzyme activities, such as peroxidase and oxidase activities [8–10]. Inspired by the pioneering nanoenzyme of Fe3O4 [11,12], graphene derivatives [13], noble metal nanoparticles [14] and transition metal-based nanoparticles [11,12], which exhibit excellent enzymatic activities, have been developed. Owing to their unique colorimetric reaction and the in situ generated hydroxide radicals (·OH), these artificial nanoenzymes can be used in biosensors [15], cancer diagnoses [16], and contaminant removal [17], among other applications. Nevertheless, it is necessary to develop more efficient nanoenzymes with a higher affinity toward substrates.

Two dimensional (2D) nanomaterials with 2D atomic crystalline structures and abundant edges can be used to improve the adsorption of reagents and the stabilization of the intermediate and subsequent redox reactions; thus, such materials can be potentially used to realize nanocatalysis [18–20]. In the 2D nanomaterial family, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) has attracted considerable attention in the optics, electrochemical catalysis, and cancer therapy domains because of its large specific surface area, strong adsorption characteristics and high reactivity [21]. In particular, MoS2 exhibits a unique peroxidase-like activity [22,23]. In particular, Zhao’s group reported that MoS2 nanosheets exhibit an inherent peroxidase-like activity, which can be used for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose [24]. Researchers have developed a series of MoS2-based nanoenzymes, such as flowerlike MoS2@MgFe2O4 [25], sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS)-modified MoS2 [22], layered MoS2-graphene and HRP nanocomposite [23], polyethylene glycol functionalized MoS2 [26], MoS2 and graphene quantum dots [27], MoS2 and Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) [28], ternary MoS2-polypyrrole-Pd [29], and MoS2-Pt3Au1 nanocomposites [30], which have demonstrated potential in realizing the colorimetric detection of H2O2. Furthermore, MoS2-based nanoenzymes have been observed to demonstrate a high catalytic activity in a wide pH range (2–7.5) [31]. However, the limit of detection (LOD) is still extremely high to realize the sensitive detection of H2O2. Although certain layered MoS2 peroxidase-like nanoenzymes exhibit a low LOD value and have been used to detect trace amounts of H2O2 in lake water, complicated synthetic steps are required, and the underlying mechanism is unclear [32]. In this context, SDS-modified MoS2 nanoparticles exhibit a negatively charged surface, resulting in a strong affinity toward the positively charged 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) [22]. However, SDS, as an anionic amphiphile, destroys a protein's native conformation through the co-assembly phenomenon and, at a low concentration, accelerates the aggregation of Aβ (1–40) via a micellar interface [33], thereby limiting its application in biosystems.

Trigonal prismatic type (2H-type) MoS2 exhibits relatively low intrinsic conductivity due to its semiconductive nature. To realize a redox reaction, an efficient electron exchange between the catalytic center and reagent is needed to ensure fast catalytic kinetics. To this end, the integration of MoS2 with 2D graphene or three dimensional (3D) conductive supports can enhance the electrocatalytic performance compared to that of the native 2H-type MoS2 [34]. Furthermore, the smart hybridization of MoS2 with Au can promote the charge transfer through the Au-S bond for the catalytic reaction, thereby facilitating the fabrication of sensitive biosensors [28,35,36]. Shu et al. reported that MoS2 nanosheet-Au nanorod hybrids can be used to develop a sensitive and reliable electrochemical biosensor to detect the H2O2 released from living cells [37]. A colorimetric method was designed to determine cholesterol in human serum by using MoS2 peroxidase-like nanoenzymes [38]. These aspects indicate that the synergistic effects between MoS2 and other active components play a key role in enhancing the enzymatic activities.

The oxidation of TMB on the surface of MoS2 requires a high fermi level to enable the electron transfer from the TMB to MoS2. Furthermore, the high affinity of MoS2 toward the substrates, such as TMB and H2O2, helps accelerate the enzymatic kinetics. In this study, we used polyoxometalates to initiate the polymerization of polypyrrole and subsequent sulfurization; subsequently, Au NPs were introduced on the outer surface to form a ternary nanocomposite (denoted as MoS2/C-Au600). This material could mimic the peroxidase catalytic activity and exhibited the typical enzyme kinetics and ping-pong mechanisms. Systematic measurements indicated that the enzymatic activity of MoS2/C-Au600 is higher than that of the previously reported peroxidase-like nanoenzymes. The synergistic coupling effect among multiple components likely led to the enhanced enzymatic activity. Hot electron transfer from Au nanoparticles to MoS2 was realized to improve the Fermi level of the nanocomposite to generate more ·OH. Furthermore, MoS2/C-Au600 was successfully used to realize the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and cancer cells.

All the solvents and chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Pyrrole solution (Py) (99%), sublimed Sulfur, HAuCl4·3H2O (99.9%), ethanol (99.5%), and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) (98%), were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Inc. (Shanghai, China). In addition, all the interference substances including NaNO3 (99%), NaF (98%), NaCl (99.5%), NaBr (99%), Na2SO4 (99%), ammonium acetate (99.99%), CaCl2 (96%), Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O (99%), KCl (99.5%), NiCl2·6H2O (98%), MgCl2·6H2O (99.99%), glucose (analytical grade), fructose (99%), urea (99%), uric acid (99%), l-histidine (99%), glycine (98.5%), l-glutamate (99%), and l-phenylalanine (99%) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Phosphomolybdic acid (H3PMo12O40·xH2O, denoted as PMo12), l-cysteine, terephthalic acid (TA), and ascorbic acid were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Limited Liability Company (Shanghai, China). Furthermore, 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (analytical purity) was provided by Xinda Chemical Factory (Harbin, China). Milli-Q purity deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm, at 25 °C) was used in the all experiments.

Morphology analyses and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of the materials were realized using the FEI Quanta 200 scanning electronic microscope (SEM) and field emission tunneling electronic microscope (TEM) (TecnaiG2F30, FEI, US), respectively. Typically, approximately 1 mg sample was sonicated in 1 mL of deionized water for 10 min, and the solution was dropped onto a Cu grid covered with a thin carbon film to perform the TEM measurements. The Raman spectra were recorded using a confocal microscope based Raman spectrometer (LabRAM XploRA, incident power of 1 mW, pumping wavelength of 532 nm). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using the Shimadzu LabX-XRD-6000 instrument with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1542 nm). The compositions and chemical states of the elements were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS, using Al (Kα) radiation as a probe). The binding energies were calibrated by considering a carbon peak (C 1s: 284.6 eV). The UV–vis spectra were obtained using an Agilent 8453 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). The fluorescence measurements were performed using the Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer F-7000 (Hitachi Ltd, Tokyo, Japan). The surface charge status was determined using a Zeta potential analyzer (Zeta PALS BI-90 Plus, Brookhaven Instruments). The Hall effect was measured using the standard Van der Pauw method applied using the SHM-3000 Hall effect tester.

MoS2/C nanospheres were synthesized as described in a previous study, albeit with slight modifications [34]. Typically, the PMo12 triggered polymerization time was set as 24 h to ensure complete polymerization (the precursor was denoted as PMo12/PPy). The sulfurization was performed in a quartz furnace with an environment involving 100 sccm argon (Ar) gas. PMo12/PPy powders and sulfur were placed in a porcelain boat in the downstream and upstream regions in the quartz tube, respectively. The evaporation temperature of sulfur was 250 °C, and the sulfurization was performed at 700 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the entire system was cooled to room temperature under an Ar environment. The sulfurized product was expressed as MoS2/C.

MoS2/C nanospheres (30 mg) were ultrasonically dispersed in 10 mL distilled water as solution A, and l-cysteine (10.92 mg) was dissolved in 90 mL distilled water as solution B. Next, 10 mL solution A was added to 90 mL solution B with stirring; the mixture solution was sonicated for 30 min. Subsequently, 600 µL HAuCl4 aqueous solution (0.025 mol/L (M)) was slowly added to the above solution with stirring. After continuous stirring for 2 h, 60 mL ascorbic acid aqueous solution (0.5 mmol/L (mM)) was added to reduce HAuCl4 into the Au NPs. The final products were centrifuged after 3 h, washed with water three times, and dried in vacuum for 12 h (Scheme 1 a). Furthermore, additional MoS2/C-Au nanocomposites using 300, 1200, and 2000 µL HAuCl4 aqueous solutions (0.025 M) were prepared according to the same procedure; the final products were termed MoS2/C-Au300, MoS2/C-Au600, MoS2/C-Au1200, and MoS2/C-Au2000, respectively.

To study the catalytic peroxidase-like properties, a blue product was generated by oxidizing TMB in the presence of H2O2. In the steady state kinetic assay experiments, using TMB as a substrate, TMB with various concentrations from 0.05 to 2.5 mM, 10 mM H2O2 and 0.1 mg/mL catalyst were added in a 0.2 M sodium acetate and acetic acid (NaAc-HAc) buffer solution (pH 4.0). In another experiment to examine the H2O2 substrate, H2O2 with various concentrations from 1 to 35 mM, 0.5 mM TMB and 0.1 mg/mL catalyst were used under the same conditions. The reaction was performed in a 3 mL quartz cuvette, and the absorption change at 652 nm was monitored using an UV–vis spectrophotometer with a recording wavelength range of 500–800 nm. The UV–vis spectra were recorded after the reaction for 5 min.

The H2O2 detection was conducted as follows: First, 0.5 mM TMB, 0.1 mg/mL catalyst and H2O2 with different concentrations were added to a 0.2 M NaAc-HAc buffer solution (3 mL, pH 4.0). The mixed solutions were heated at 60 °C in a water bath for 5 min. Finally, the absorbance of the mixed solution was analyzed at 652 nm. The final concentration of H2O2 in the system ranged from 1 to 1000 μM.

In an experiment, 5 × 104 HepG2 cells were seeded in a 96 well and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2. Subsequently, the cells were rinsed with PBS three times. The MoS2/C-Au600 (0.1 mg/mL) solution in a culture medium was added and incubated for another 4 h. Subsequently, a certain volume of H2O2 (10 mM) and TMB (0.5 mM) was added to each well. After incubation for 10 min, the cells were rinsed with PBS to remove the unendocytosed MoS2/C-Au600, excessive H2O2 (10 mM) and TMB. The colored HepG2 cells were observed using the Olympus microscope. The physical adsorption and endocytosis of HepG2 cells toward MoS2/C-Au600 was evaluated after 4 h of incubation; subsequently, the cells were gently rinsed, digested, and boiled in concentrated nitric acid. Finally, the element content of Au was determined using ICP (iCAP 7400).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of MoS2/C-Au nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: The formation principle and morphology of the PMo12/PPy nanospheres, as shown in Scheme 1a, have been described in our previous work [34]. The hybrid nanospheres were topologically transformed into solid spheres when annealed at 700 °C in the presence of sulfur. The curved MoS2 nanosheets were randomly distributed in the hybrid nanospheres, as shown in Fig. 1 a and 1b. Low resolution TEM images of the MoS2/C and MoS2/C-Au600 nanospheres, as shown in Fig. 1a and 1c, respectively, indicated that the morphology remained after modification with Au nanoparticles, exhibiting an average diameter of ~ 93 nm. The high resolution TEM image shown in Fig. 1d indicated that the Au NPs with an average size of ~2.1 nm (Fig. S1) were uniformly anchored on the MoS2/C nanosphere. The EDS spectra of MoS2/C and MoS2/C-Au600 shown in Fig. S2 indicated the coexistence of C, O, Mo and S in both the cases, and the atomic ratio of Mo:S was noted to be approximately 1:2. In addition, the newly appeared Au peak in the latter sample indicated the successful incorporation of Au. As shown in Fig. S3, the Raman spectrum exhibited the characteristic peaks of MoS2 at 381 and 404 cm−1, which can be attributed to the E2g and A1g peaks, respectively; these findings are consistent with those reported in previous work [39,40]. In addition, the intensities of these two bands in the MoS2/C-Au600 nanospheres were enhanced by 130% in comparison with that of MoS2/C, indicating the occurrence of a chemical interaction or bonding between the MoS2 and Au NPs [41]. D, G and 2D bands for carbon were observed at 1379.9, 1553.3 and 2862.1 cm−1, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1e. The XRD patterns shown in Fig. 1f confirmed the presence of MoS2 (PDF#74–0923) in the MoS2/C-Au600 nanosphere. The peak of ~ 14.4°, corresponding to the (0 0 2) plane of MoS2, was not present due to its ultrathin stacking nanostructure in the nanosphere [42–44]. In addition to the MoS2 peak, the characteristic peaks for (1 1 1), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) crystalline facets of Au (PDF#04–0784) were observed at 38.1°, 64.7° and 77.4°, respectively. The XPS shown in Fig. 2 a also indicated the presence of Au, Mo, S, N and C elements in the MoS2/C-Au600 nanospheres. In summary, the experimental results verified that the Au NPs were successfully attached to the MoS2/C nanosphere [45].

The fine deconvolution of the Mo 3d XPS spectrum for MoS2/C, as shown in Fig. 2b, indicated the main peaks of Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2 at a binding energy of 232.4 and 229.2 eV for the Mo(IV) oxidation state, respectively. A small peak was also observed at the binding energy of 235.7 eV, which could be attributed to the Mo(VI) oxidation state. Another peak at a binding energy of 226.4 eV was ascribed to S 2s. In contrast, the binding energy of Mo 3d and S 2p in MoS2/C-Au600 (Fig. 2c) decreased by approximately 0.20 and 0.15 eV in comparison to those of MoS2/C [46], likely indicating the electron transfer from the Au nanoparticle to MoS2/C. As shown in Fig. 2d, Au 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 peaks appeared at the binding energies of 84.0 eV and 87.7 eV, respectively, indexed to Au(0), and two other peaks were noted at binding energies of 84.6 eV and 88.4 eV, respectively, ascribed to Au(1). Thus, the presence of ascorbic acid could reduce the AuCl4 - into Au NPs [47]. The Au loading in MoS2/C-Au600 was measured as approximately 3.93 atom %.

To verify the peroxidase-like activity of MoS2/C-Au600, TMB was selected as a colorimetric substrate, which underwent oxidation reactions in the presence of H2O2. The charge transfer complex that ultimately produced the amine and diimine oxidation products and the maximum absorption peak at 652 nm was related to the TMB oxidation products (Scheme 1b). Because the properties of nanoenzymes and biological enzymes are similar, their catalytic activity is influenced by the pH values of the buffer solution. Therefore, we first checked the pH dependence, as shown in Fig. 3 a, and observed that the relative activity, namely, the absorbance intensity, gradually increased as the pH increased from 2 to 4; the absorbance intensity reached its maximum value at a pH of 4 and decreased at higher pH conditions. This result was consistent with those of HRP and several other peroxidase-like nanoenzymes [4]. This phenomenon was ascribed to the fast decomposition of H2O2 into O2 under high pH conditions, leading to insufficient ·OH radical amounts to realize the TMB oxidation. Furthermore, the active sites of Au or Mo deteriorated under acidic conditions (pH less than 4), thereby affecting the activation of the H2O2 and subsequent TMB oxidation. It can be observed from Fig. 3b that a higher temperature favored the activation of the peroxidase-like activity until 60 °C. However, the relative activity retention was approximately 80% at 70 °C, owing to the self-decomposition of H2O2 at a high temperature. A natural enzyme such as HRP would be completely denatured under such a high temperature. Therefore, the optimal pH value and temperature for our experiment were 4 and 60 °C, respectively.

To evaluate the peroxidase-like activity of MoS2/C-Au600, six different catalytic systems were evaluated under the optimal experimental conditions, as shown in Fig. 3c. The characteristic peak at 652 nm was not observed in the catalytic systems of TMB (curve 1), TMB + MoS2/C (curve 3) and TMB + MoS2/C-Au600 (curve 4), indicating the necessity of the presence of H2O2, and no blue product was observed (inset of Fig. 3c). In the TMB + H2O2 catalytic system (curve 2), a weak absorption peak at 652 nm was observed due to the ability of H2O2 to spontaneously generate ·OH radicals; this observation is consistent with the findings of a previous study [22]. In contrast, a strong absorption peak was observed in the MoS2/C + TMB + H2O2 catalytic system (curve 5), as confirmed by the apparent blue color resulting from the one electron oxidation of the TMB (inset of Fig. 3c) [32]. Furthermore, the strongest absorption intensity at 652 nm was observed in the MoS2/C-Au600 + TMB + H2O2 catalytic system (curve 6), also indicated by the deep blue color noted in the inset of Fig. 3c. The peroxidase-like activity of MoS2/C-Au600 was further examined by monitoring the time dependent absorbance changes at 652 nm, as shown in Fig. 3d. The absorbance intensity at 652 nm increased as a function of the reaction time in curve 6, and the intensity was stronger than those in other catalytic systems for a given reaction time, indicating the superior peroxidase-like activity of MoS2/C-Au600.

Moreover, the effect of the size and loading of the Au NP on the peroxidase-like activity was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S4, the diameter of the Au NPs (~2.0 nm) was almost unchanged in MoS2/C-Au300 in comparison with that in MoS2/C-Au600 (~2.1 nm). When the amount of HAuCl4 was increased to 1200 µL and 2000 µL, larger Au NPs with a diameter of ~ 8.0 nm (MoS2/C-Au1200 in Figs. S5a and S5b) and dendritic Au clusters with a diameter of ~21.9 nm (MoS2/C-Au2000 in Figs. S5c and S5d) were formed on the surface of the MoS2/C, respectively. As shown in Fig. S6, the relative activity of MoS2/C-Au300 decreased in comparison with that of MoS2/C-Au600 owing to the lower absorbance under the same experiment conditions. According to the TEM result shown in Fig. S4, the diameter of the Au NPs in MoS2/C-Au300 was similar to that of the Au NPs in MoS2/C-Au600, and the lower activity was directly related to the low content of Au; specifically, fewer Au NPs contributed to the synergistic effect with MoS2/C in the case of MoS2/C-Au300. At a higher Au loading, the relative activity of MoS2/C-Au1200 was inferior to that of MoS2/C-Au600, as shown in Fig. S6a, likely owing the aggregation of the Au NPs. However, the peroxidase-likeperoxidase-like activity of MoS2/C-Au2000 was comparable to that of MoS2/C-Au600, as shown in Fig. S6b. Although aggregation was still observed when excessive Au precursor was used to synthesize MoS2/C-Au2000, the dendritic nanostructures favored the oxidation reaction of TMB due to its exposed active sites; this outcome is consistent with the findings of previous work [48].

To examine the stability of the catalytic system of MoS2/C-Au600 + TMB + H2O2, certain interferents were added in the reaction solutions. These interferents included some common anions and cations, such as NO3 −, F−, Cl−, Br−, SO4 2−, NH4 +, Ca2+, Zn2+, K+, Ni2+ and Mg2+, as well as glucose, urea, uric acid, l-histidine, glycine, cystine, glutamate, and phenylalanine (50 μM). The effect of these potential interferents is illustrated in Fig. S7: The absorbance intensity was relatively unchanged after the addition of the interferents. Overall, the interference effect was negligible in all the cases, indicating the stable enzyme mimetic activity of MoS2/C-Au600.

The kinetic parameters of the peroxidase-like reactions were measured using the enzyme kinetic theory and approach. As shown in Fig. 4 a and 4b, the oxidation reactions with different concentrations of the TMB and H2O2 catalyzed by MoS2/C corresponded to the typical Michaelis–Menten curves. The Michaelis–Menten constant (Km ) and maximum reaction velocity (Vmax ) were determined using the Michaelis–Menten equation
V 0

(1), in which V0 is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximum reaction velocity, [S] is the substrate concentration, and Km is the Michaelis–Menten constant [49]. In principle, a smaller Km and higher Vmax can ensure a stronger affinity with the substrates, as well as a higher catalytic efficiency [50]. The double reciprocal Michaelis–Menten curves were fitted according to the Lineweaver–Burk equation

(2), as shown in Fig. 4c and 4d. As indicated in Table 1 , the Km value of MoS2/C with TMB (0.224 mM) was lower than that of HRP (0.43 mM). Moreover, the Km value of MoS2/C was 8.11 mM when H2O2 was used as a substrate, and this value was larger than that of HRP (3.7 mM). The lower Km value of MoS2/C indicated a higher affinity to the TMB molecule compared to the corresponding affinity of the HRP [51]. The typical Michaelis–Menten curves were also obtained by varying the concentrations of H2O2 and TMB for MoS2/C-Au600, as shown in Fig. 5 a and 5b, respectively. Km and Vmax were derived from the double reciprocal Michaelis–Menten curves shown in Fig. 5c and 5d, respectively, according to equation 2. Notably, the Km values for MoS2/C-Au600 were approximately 0.104 mM and 2.43 mM when TMB and H2O2 were used as the substrates, respectively, and these values are smaller than those of MoS2/C (0.224 mM and 8.11 mM).

These results indicate that the introduction of Au on the surface of MoS2/C considerably improved the affinity toward TMB and H2O2. To further clarify the mechanism for the strong affinity toward TMB, the zeta potential was measured. As shown in Fig. S8, the zeta potential of MoS2/C was −8.7 mV ± 0.8 mV, which decreased to −10.6 mV ± 1.3 mV for MoS2/C-Au600. In general, positively charged TMB molecules (as amino groups are protonated at pH 4.0) prefer to adsorb on the more negatively charged surface of MoS2/C-Au600, resulting in a strong binding ability toward TMB. The parallel slopes in Fig. 4c and 4d, as well as in Fig. 5c and 5d, indicated that this enzymatic reaction followed the ping-pong mechanism [52]. In other words, the MoS2/C or MoS2/C-Au600 combined with the first substrate and reacted, releasing the first product before reacting with the second substrate. In addition to the small Km value, MoS2/C-Au600 also exhibited Vmax values of 4.54 × 10-7 M s−1 and 3.73 × 10-7 M s−1 when TMB and H2O2 were used as the substrates, respectively; these values were higher than those of MoS2/C (2.28 × 10-7 M s−1 and 1.89 × 10-7 M s−1), and most existing nanoenzymes, listed in Table 1, indicating the high reaction velocity of MoS2/C-Au600. According to the Michaelis–Menten equation, Vmax equals the reaction velocity when the nanoenzyme is fully covered by the substrates. In this case, the electron transfer between nanoenzyme and substrates plays a key role in boosting the TMB oxidation reaction, assisted by the H2O2. In principle, the thermal electron injection of Au nanoparticles toward MoS2/C can enhance the electron density state around the Fermi level of MoS2. Such a synergistic effect can effectively accelerate the electron transfer from the MoS2 nanosheet to H2O2, leading to the formation of more ·OH radicals to oxidize the TMB on the surface of MoS2/C-Au600 [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of peroxidase-like activity of MoS2/C-Au600
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation of TMB to generate blue oxidation products occurs due to the ·OH radicals. MoS2/C-Au600 can adsorb H2O2 due to its high affinity, which is effectively activated to form ·OH owing to the synergistic effects of all the components; subsequently, the TMB can be oxidized to form the blue product [53]. To confirm this mechanism, TA was used as a fluorescent probe in the MoS2/C-Au600 system. TA itself does not have fluorescence; however, a strong fluorescent compound 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA) is formed when TA reacts with ·OH [55]. The corresponding chemical process is illustrated in Fig. 6 a. The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range of 350–600 nm at an excitation wavelength of 324 nm (Fig. 6b) [32]. The results indicated that reaction systems (1) and (2) exhibited nearly no fluorescence, and reaction system (3) exhibited a weak fluorescence. In contrast, reaction system (4) exhibited the strongest fluorescence. In particular, when MoS2/C-Au600 is added to a solution both containing TA and H2O2, a large amount of ·OH radicals are generated to react with the nonluminescent TA to obtain high fluorescence HTA.

These results indicate that the MoS2/C-Au600 exhibits a higher peroxidase-like activity than that of the benchmark groups. To eliminate the oxidase like activities, MoS2/C or MoS2/C-Au600 was directly used to oxidize TMB; however, the results were negative. It was considered that the TMB can only be oxidized by the considered catalysts with the assistance of H2O2. A TA fluorescence probe was used to identify the presence of ·OH in the given peroxidase-like reaction. The highest peroxidase-like activity of MoS2/C-Au600 can be explained as follows. The lone pair of electrons in the amino group of TMB is expected to be effectively accepted by the catalytic sites [56]. In the absence of the catalyst, H2O2 itself is not sufficiently active to completely oxidize TMB, as confirmed by the weak absorption peak at 652 nm, as shown in Fig. 3a. As indicated by the red arrow in Scheme 2 , the electrons in TMB cannot be directly transferred to the H2O2. Therefore, the ·OH radicals derived from the H2O2 can be considered as the real active species for the oxidation of TMB [57,58]. In principle, if the catalyst possesses a higher Fermi level to realize an efficient electron transfer to H2O2 to form ·OH radicals, more active species can be efficiently generated for the oxidation of TMB [59]. From the viewpoint of solid-state physics, the thermal excitation of Au NPs can occur spontaneously at the heterointerface between MoS2/C and Au [46]. The work function of Au is approximately 5.1 eV smaller than that of MoS2 (~5.23–5.26 eV) [60]. Thus, the thermal electrons in the Au NPs can transfer to the surrounding MoS2 nanosheets due to the favorable energy diagram, small Schottky barrier energy and small contact resistance. It has been indicated that the Schottky barrier energy between the MoS2 and Au ranges from 0.06 eV to 0.16 eV, and the contact resistance is approximately 42 Ω.mm [61]. Therefore, the thermal electrons can be effectively transferred, thereby rendering the Fermi level of MoS2/C-Au600 to higher than that of the unmodified MoS2/C. The increased Fermi level of MoS2/C-Au600 facilitates the production of OH radicals, thereby promoting the oxidation of TMB [62]. This assumption was proven by performing a Hall experiment, as shown in Fig. S9. The average carrier mobility for the MoS2/C-Au600 film was approximately (2.08 ± 0.21) × 1015 higher than that of MoS2/C film ((9.67 ± 0.36) × 1013). Therefore, as anticipated, in the MoS2/C + TMB + H2O2 catalytic system (Fig. 3c and 3d, curve 5), a significant absorption change was observed at 652 nm, confirming the peroxidase-like activity of MoS2/C. Furthermore, the MoS2/C-Au600 + TMB + H2O2 catalytic system, as shown in Fig. 3c and 3d (curve 6), yielded the largest absorbance at 652 nm due to the synergistic effect between MoS2/C and Au.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 using MoS2/C-Au600 as peroxidase mimetics
ce:section.ce:para:
H2O2 is representative of the reactive oxygen species (ROS) and has thus received extensive attention. The detection of H2O2 is extremely important to ensure human health. Considering the peroxidase-like properties of MoS2/C-Au600, a colorimetric method to detect H2O2 was designed using the optimal experimental conditions described in the previous section. Various concentrations of H2O2 aliquots were added to the NaAc-HAc buffer solution (pH = 4.0) containing TMB and catalysts, and H2O2 was detected by monitoring the change in the absorbance at 652 nm in the UV–vis spectrum. Fig. 7 a and 7d show the increase in absorbance at 652 nm with an increase in the H2O2 concentration, catalyzed by MoS2/C and MoS2/C-Au600, respectively. The solution colors as a function of the H2O2 concentrations in these two catalytic systems are displayed in Fig. S10. To obtain the detection range and LOD values, the absorbance intensities vs·H2O2 concentrations were plotted, as shown in Fig. 7b and 7e for the two catalytic systems. Fig. 7c shows a linear calibration curve for MoS2/C; the curve can be noted to be linear from 2 × 10-5 M to 2 × 10-4 M (R2 = 0.99)·H2O2 can be well detected in this range, and LOD is estimated to be approximately 2.99 μM (
L O D = K
S 0
/ S
, where K is a numerical factor selected according to the desired confidence level; S0 is the standard deviation of the blank measurements (n = 10, K = 3) and S is the slope of the calibration curve) [63]. In contrast, the linear range in the case of MoS2/C-Au600, as shown in Fig. 7f, is 1 × 10-5 M to 2 × 10-4 M (R2 = 0.99), and the detection limit is 1.82 μM. Compared to the reported values, the colorimetric strategy based on the peroxidase-likeperoxidase-like properties MoS2/C-Au600 is comparable to the existing methods (Table 2 ).

H2O2 plays a key role in many biochemical reactions, such as the metabolism of proteins and carbohydrates [68,69], and it can be used as an indicator of many critical diseases such as cancer [70,71]. Early diagnoses of cancer can effectively reduce the cancer mortality. Until now, various cancer cell detection strategies such as fluorescence [72], electrochemical [73], chemiluminescence [74] and colorimetric detection [68,75] approaches have been developed. However, most of these methods are expensive, time consuming and tedious for practical medical applications. In contrast, nanoenzymes can be conveniently prepared with an easily controllable structure and composition. The abovementioned data indicate that MoS2/C-Au600 with peroxidase-like activity can image cancer cells in the presence of TMB and H2O2. Cancer cells exhibit an enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect against nanomaterials of a certain size (below 100 nm) that is different from that of normal cells; this aspect has proven to be a “royal gate” in drug delivery. For instance, Wang et al. used the EPR effect to deliver tungsten suboxide for photoacoustic imaging guided cancer therapy [76]. Moreover, Chen’s group observed the effective accumulation of tannic acid-Fe nanoparticles due to the EPR effect of tumor tissues [77].

These analyses indicate that, even though no antibody is modified on the surface of MoS2/C-Au600, it can spontaneously accumulate on the surface or inside the cancer cells due to the EPR effect. Furthermore, the pKa of TMB is ~4.2, and it can thus carry partially positively charged amino groups at pH 4.0, favoring the adsorption on the negatively charged surface of a cancer cell. In a typical process of cancer cell detection, MoS2/C-Au600 is first accumulated on the surface or partially inside the cancer cell. Subsequently, driven by the electrostatic interaction between the positive TMB and negative cancer cell, the TMB molecules are adsorbed on the surface of the cancer cell and oxidized into blue products upon the addition of H2O2 [78]. The adsorbed MoS2/C-Au600 and intracellular amount can be quantified through the ICP measurement, and the EDS and XPS results can be attained. In this work, the Au loading amount was approximately 9% by weight according to the XPS result. Thus, the overall MoS2/C-Au600 loading amount on the HepG2 cells was approximately 23.2 ± 0.4 ng/103 cells. As shown in Fig. 8 a, in the presence of only TMB and H2O2 (without MoS2/C-Au600), the cancer cells were barely seen. In contrast, blue colored cancer cells could be separated after incubation with MoS2/C-Au600 and TMB and subsequent addition of H2O2. This result demonstrates that the peroxidase-like activity of MoS2/C-Au600 can be used to probe cancer cells.

An efficient peroxidase-like nanoenzyme was synthesized by anchoring Au NPs on the surface of MoS2/C via polyoxometalate assisted polymerization, sulfurization and mild reduction reactions. The experiment results showed that the prepared MoS2/C-Au600 displayed peroxidase-like activity, as the Km values were approximately 0.104 mM and 2.43 mM when TMB and H2O2 were used as the substrates, respectively, indicating the higher affinity of the MoS2/C-Au600 to substrates compared to that of natural HRP and previously reported nanoenzymes such as MoS2/PPy nanocomposite (0.41 mM and 12.8 mM) [56], 2D ultrathin nanosheets of Co/Al (0.372 mM and 22.13 mM) [66], ficin (0.14 mM and 0.58 mM) [79], and Co3O4/CeO2 (0.36 mM and 132.21 mM) [80]. It is assumed that the thermal electron injection from the Au nanoparticles to MoS2/C improved the Fermi level of the nanocomposites, thereby facilitating the electron donating ability toward H2O2 to generate ·OH radicals to realize the oxidation of TMB. MoS2/C-Au600 exhibited typical Michaelis–Menten enzymatic kinetics. The concentration range of H2O2 detection for MoS2/C-Au600 was from 1 × 10-5 M to 2 × 10-4 M, larger than that of MoS2/C. Furthermore, the LOD value was 1.82 µM, smaller than that of Fe3O4 (3 μM) [64], N-GODs (5.3 μM) [65], MoS2/PPy (45 μM) [56], Co/Al (10 μM) [66], and Au@Ag (6 μM) [67]. A larger number of ·OH radicals were generated in the case of MoS2/C-Au600 compared to that for MoS2/C, as confirmed by the fluorescence probe test. In addition, by exploiting the superior peroxidase-like activity of MoS2/C-Au600, the prepared materials could be used to visualize cancer cells by conducting colorimetric immunoassays. This work not only provides a basis for the preparation of nanoenzymes by amplifying the synergistic effect among all the components but also highlights the importance of improving the amount of the ·OH radicals generated to realize sensitive H2O2 detection and the colorimetric immunoassay of cancer cells.
